prevalence.source: Orphanet rare disease summaries; Indian inborn errors of metabolism (IEM) center reports
prevalence.trend: stable; diagnosed prevalence increasing with greater IEM awareness and MS/MS testing

incidence.source: IEM incidence syntheses; tertiary metabolic clinic series (India)

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare), but severe per-patient morbidity without early management
burden.mortalityRate: Higher in undiagnosed neonatal-onset; reduced with early intervention
burden.source: Orphanet/clinical outcomes and modeled rare disease context

vulnerablePopulations: patients without access to metabolic genetics services
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Metabolic acidosis/hyperammonemia in crises; elevated methylmalonic acid; MS/MS acylcarnitine profile (C3); confirmatory genetics; assess B12 responsiveness

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: limited; expanded newborn screening not universal and confirmatory testing is centralized
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Hundreds to a few thousand (rare; underdiagnosed)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; best in centers with metabolic teams and access to medical nutrition

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Diagnosis clustered in tertiary pediatric hospitals; many cases present late after metabolic crisis
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emergency decompensation management, protein-restricted diet/medical formulas, carnitine, B12 if responsive, renal/neurologic monitoring
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Limited newborn screening coverage, travel burden to specialty centers, cost and supply of metabolic formulas
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Growing network of IEM centers and increasing access to MS/MS testing in large cities
